sentences.text: Need a mic, too, huh?
Oh.
Well, this town, Comlausa, you're not going to find it on any map.
It's a small village, which consisted of 26 Jewish families.
Maybe the entire village, maybe were about 200, maybe, families all together.
Most of them were Romanian people.
My family-- we were eight in my immediate family.
My-- my parents-- mother, father-- and I had four brothers and a sister.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was born in 1928.
And at that time, this village was part of Romania.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- the village had one old schoolhouse, which was rebuilt in the mid-'30s.
I remember going to the old one and also to the new one.
But most of our-- the emphasis was not put on-- on-- on secular education.
The emphasis was put more on the Yiddish education.
In other words, we had a cheder there.
And even so we were only 26 families, my village always prided itself of having one of the best cheders for the entire area.
As a matter of fact, there were villages which are much larger than my village.
They would send their children to my village.
We had two cheders.
One cheder was [HEBREW] Melamed.
This Melamed, his name was Reb Moishele.
He was a small fellow, and he hardly made a living.
But they would pay, it was called [HEBREW].
They would pay so much for a season to send the children there to-- to study.
I believe we started-- when you were 3 years old, you know, they used to shave off your head.
And then you had Peahs.
So they went to the-- they went to cheder, to Moishele.
Moishele, and his wife's name was [?
Fega. ?] And there we studied [? all ?] [? of ?] [? base, ?] oh, until the age of about 7, 8 years old.
From there, we went to another cheder, where this was more advanced.
And even so, when I was 9 years, 10 years old, my-- see, over there, there were no classes.
You went to a cheder to-- everybody went to the same class, to the same class.
But the studies-- the-- everybody studied as much as his capacity could-- could-- could-- could absorb.
My friends who studied with me, even so when I was 10 years old, they were three, four years older than me.
Not ?] [? with-- ?] I had tremendous capacity to study the weekly Seder, [HEBREW].
And not only did we have to study it from the book, but we had to know it by heart.
Cames Thursday afternoon, you knew the Gemara, Gemara [HEBREW], with all the commentaries, by heart.
And I still remember it because if you didn't know it Thursday, they would beat you mercilessly.
If they would do the same in the United States, that teacher would be locked up.
But over there, apparently, maybe it was a good system because otherwise, I wouldn't have remembered it even to this day.
Whatever I know-- and I want you to know that I went to-- to cheder, to Jewish schools, until the age of 12.
And I think I'm capable of-- of having a conversation with-- with any rabbi, when it comes to Talmud and to-- and to Jewish studies.
And apparently-- apparently-- the system must've been-- must have had something good.
So most-- again, most of the concentration of my village was strictly Yiddish.
In other words, over there, when it came a Shabbos or a Rosh Hashanah, or a Pesach, you didn't have to look at the calendar what-- what-- when it's going to be Pesach.
You felt it.
It was in the air.
It was in the air, every holiday.
We had a synagogue in Comlausa where they send over three men that was at, like-- like-- during World War I to America.
And they made money here.
And they send it back.
They saved it up.
And with that money, that was the seed money to build a shul, a synagogue.
The people-- everybody got involved in building it.
Of course, it was built before I was born.
It was finished in 1925.
But the people, they went to the mountains, they-- they-- they-- to cut stone and wood.
And they worked there for years and years until they build this up.
The walls were about a meter thick, which is over 3 feet thick, the walls.
High ceilings.
On the ceilings there was painted stars.
Around the-- around-- around the synagogue, on the wall, they used to have the-- the zodiac-- the zodiac.
Every month had a, you know, like the fish, the chicken, the whatever it was.
It had all around, all around, all around the-- the main shul, the main synagogue.
Next, what we had what is called a [HEBREW], a [HEBREW].
So in the-- in the wintertime, when it was very, very cold, so you didn't have to heat up the whole shul, so you just went into the [HEBREW].
Next to it, we had another building where the shammash used to live.
Over there was the cheder.
I mean, for the-- for the advanced study cheder.
It was also used for Pesach to bake matzahs because it had a big oven in one room.
And they have the people who used to bake the matzahs.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, how they baked matzahs over there, this is a story by itself.
And it's something very hard for American mind to understand the-- the-- how-- how the system worked.
If you don't mind, if I have a minute, I would like to describe this because this is a very interesting story.
[DRINKS] When they had the grain, the wheat, and what you make matzahs, women would get together from house to house.
And they would pour the wheat on the table.
And they would select each kernel.
All the impurities was taken out.
And then they put it in a bag, all the wheat.
Then the men would get together, and they would go to a mill.
It was a water mill, which was driven by water.
They would take off the stone.
And they would chop the bottom of the stone in order to take out all the old flour, so you shouldn't cause chametz, what they call chametz.
So they would chop the stone.
They'd chip it until they took off all the stone-- all-- all the-- the pieces from the stone and all-- what everything is-- which was connected to it.
Then they-- they would mill it.
And after it was milled, brought back to the village.
The-- by the way, this mill was in next village, a village called [?
Batarci. ?]
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then everybody in my village had a job.
For an example, I would give water, to put the water into the-- into the flour.
I was called a [HEBREW].
Somebody would give the flour in another site.
He would-- he was called a [HEBREW].
In the middle, there was Reb Shmiel.
He was a small guy, with a long white beard.
And he would knead the dough.
But it had to be done very fast because if you let the dough stay there, so the dough would rise, and it wouldn't be kosher.
So he did it very fast.

Well, Passover time, we used to find a lot of hair from Shmiel's beard inside the-- inside the-- inside with the matzahs, you know, because he was kneading it so fast, he's-- even his-- you know, piece of the beard would fall in.
But that was OK.
It was kosher.
After they took out the dough from Shmiel's, you know, when he was finished kneading it, there was a long table.
And somebody stood there.
He was known as a [HEBREW].
In other words, he would rip off a piece of the dough and gave it to the woman.
He was known as the [HEBREW].
Woman would stay in line in either side of the table.
Maybe there were about 25, 30 woman.
And they would roll out the matzah.
After the matzah was rolled out, somebody would take it over to another table, where it would go through a wheel-- a wheel full of spokes, so when the matzah heats up, it shouldn't blow up.
So the matzah has-- I think even-- even today's matzah has little spokes in it.
That's the reasoning.
Even today's matzah has it.
They would-- they would roll over with a-- a little wheel with spokes.
He was known as a reddler.
Then somebody would take the matzah and put it on a-- what do you call that rolling stone-- the rolling stick?
There is a name for it.
A rolling pin.
A rolling pin, yeah.
Put it on a rolling pin and bring it over to the-- to the oven.
He was known as a [HEBREW].
But the guy by the oven, he was known as a shiber.
So he would take it and throw it into the oven.
He make-- I would say that from the time you take the-- the-- the flour to the time the matzah comes out, maybe-- maybe it was 14, 15 minutes, the whole-- the whole thing.
And this was a community undertaking.
And I want you to know that this used to take weeks, weeks to go with the wheat.
It-- but it was a project.
It was a big project.
It wasn't like today, you go into a Waldbaum's, and you say, give me 3 pounds of whatever-- you know, matzahs.
There, you-- you-- you worked for it.
And you appreciated it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after-- all right, this was Passover, mostly Passover.
Also, Passover was the holiday where if somebody got a new suit or a new pair of shoes or whatever, it was for Passover.
That was the time when-- when-- when the kids used to get new clothes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] During the High Holy Days-- Shovuos, for example.
After Passover comes Shovuos.
Now Shovuos is that holiday when-- when-- when Jews are reminded that they received the Torah.
In every house, they used to go out and tear off branches from walnut trees because the walnut tree is in bloom.
And it has a very good smell.
And they would put in the house full of branches with walnut trees.
This supposedly is to remind the Jews that they were-- were standing on Mount Sinai because it looked like a-- like a mountain there, you know.
Rosh Hashanah-- even so we were a small village, we used to hire the best [HEBREW] the best [HEBREW] in the area because we appreciated a good chazzan.
And as a matter of fact, to this day, I am the [HEBREW] here in the Shelter Rock Jewish Center, which is a modern Orthodox-- I mean, a modern conservative congregation.
We have about 700 members in our congregation.
And I'm the [HEBREW].
And I still daven on the same nusach, on the same-- the way I remember it from my hometown.
And it's surprising to know that many time, young people walk over to me.
And they say, you know something, I really appreciate the way you-- the way you daven because it has a certain Jewish [HEBREW] to it which was squeezed out in America.
I don't know what happened to it.
But it's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yom Kippur.
Night.
I want you to know that our village didn't have no electricity.
As a matter of fact, the first time they brought in electricity to my village was in the '80s, maybe about 15 years ago, for the first time that my village was electrified.
And so in shul, they used to-- every family would come with a huge candle, about 3 feet high.
And there was a table.
And they would mount those candles on the table.
So when you walked into shul in Yom Kipper night, everything was dark-- [HEBREW] night, I'm talking about.
But you see about 26 candles in the main.
It gave it a certain flavor.
It gave it a certain holiness, a certain awe.
The woman, most of them, were dressed in white.
Everybody in the family was in shul.
Some of the man stayed in shul all night long until Yom Kippur was over.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was one time when I went to the rabbi.
Well, see, my village was a small village.
But a lot of people used to go to the rebbe.
And I remember once, me and my father, we went down to the Spinka Rebbe for Yom Kippur.
It was a big thing for the Hasidic-- I wouldn't say we were Hasidic.
But over there, everybody belonged to a rabbi.
This rebbe, by the way, was not only a rebbe, a teacher.
He was also a miracle maker.
If you had a problem in business, in business, you went to the rebbe.
You were sick, you went to the rebbe.
I remember my father once-- it's just a thought which I have right now-- he went to the rebbe, you know, because he was a farmer.
And-- and-- and when he brought in things from the-- from the farm, was-- it was-- there was hardly anything-- anything-- you know, the-- it was a very poor harvest.
And my father was-- we had six children.
So he went to the rebbe.
And he asked the rebbe what to do because he wouldn't have enough bread for the children.
And the rebbe told him to buy two sheep because he told him, from sheep, you get wool, you get milk, you could get meat.
And sheep is going to be a blessing.
So he bought two sheeps.
He borrowed money.
He bought two sheep.
I think about-- took about three, four months, and the two sheep died.
So could-- couldn't-- and my father was a very successful businessmen, as you could see.
But after that, he bought a goat.
And to tell you the story about a goat, see, I think we'll need another interview for that.
But it's a-- well-- What I want to bring out is people over there, if they had enough bread, because everybody grew their own bread, you know, from year to year, it was considered rich.
A lot of Jewish people were like sharecroppers.
They would go out and-- and-- and work for somebody else and get a portion of the-- of the-- of the produce.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember, there was one woman in my village.
As a matter of fact, it's the village next to mine.
It's called [NON-ENGLISH].
There was only one Jewish family living there.
And that summer was a very bad summer.
It was raining all the time.
And the corn-- we grew corn.
By the way, our main bread was corn, not wheat.
We used to make cornbread.
I still like it to this day.
And I passed by.
And the corn was very wet.
It wasn't dry.
So she drew-- she-- she dried the corn in an oven.
Her name was Rivka, and she had 14 children.
Two of them survived.
They live in Israel.
And I'll never forget-- and I was a kid.
You know, and she told me, [HEBREW].
That means, if the Messiah doesn't come by Thursday, I really don't know what we are going to eat.
In other words, they had enough maybe for a week.
So what I'm bringing out here is I can't say it was poor because I really never felt poverty.
Never.
Never did I feel poor.
But we were below what they call today the poverty line, that's for sure.
Anyway, coming back to my family, as I said we had-- we had-- I had four brothers and a sister.
And we had also a cow, you know.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were OK.
I remember-- I remember, there were some happy-- I remember [? a bunch of ?] happy-- I remember a lot of happy times there, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
My father, as I say, was a farmer.
He was born in an-- he was-- he was born in a village-- in a village.
And he was adopted to a family in my village when he was 3 years old.
And he grew up in my village.
And my mother, she was the most beautiful girl in the whole area.
But my father, when he grew up, he, you know, he wanted to marry her.
But of course, she was a very beautiful girl.
By the way, that story was told to me that she hesitated.
So my grandmother took her broom and polished her up on one Saturday afternoon, and she agreed to marry him.
But we had six children.
She married when she was 17 years old.
She was then 16.
She was 16.
When she was 17 years old-- when she was 17 years old, I-- I was born.
And as a matter of fact, when I came into the camps, my mother was-- she was like 33, 34 years old.
And so this is how we lived until-- until 19-- I mean, this is how it was.
Then, I remember in the '30s that we had a lot of problems then in Romania because there was-- there were politics.
There was the Iron Guard.
The Iron Guard was trying to take over the government.
The Iron Guard were the Fascist Party.
In those days, the Iron Guard consisted of Cuza, Vaida, Octavius Goga, Zelea Codreanu.
They were the big Nazis there in Romania.
Then they had the Liberal Party.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And then they had the Socialist Party.
The leader of the Socialist Party was [NON-ENGLISH].
They were like the-- the most democrat-- more democratic.
And once the fascists took over-- I think it was in 1937-- and we would go to shul every Monday.
And every Monday and Thursday, we would fast and recite [HEBREW] because we were in big trouble because they had in mind to liquidate the Jews, to burn down the shuls.
And they were the fascists there.
But a miracle happened.
The king had them all arrested and had them all executed.
So in my village, I really don't remember any overt anti-Semitism because there were mostly Romanian people there.
And I-- and I really-- we got along very good-- very good with-- I mean, they were friends of mine, the-- when I went to school, they were my friends.
The people living in Comlausa, they were-- they-- they would work for my father.
My father had-- they were-- you know, we-- we socialized.
We-- we-- they were plain peasants.
And we had sometimes problems, too.
Sometimes, it was men from the city.
When I told you, in the mid-'30s, 1937, when those fascists, they came into the city.
And they had like a pogrom, where they would go to Jewish shuls.
And-- and they would beat up-- they would beat up people on the street.
So they came to-- to the villages there.
And I remember, it was like a holiday like, Sukkos.
And when they came to the next village, to Batarci, somebody from the Jewish in Batarci send over an emissary to Comlausa that the fascists are coming to my village.
Well, I remembered, we-- all the people from the shul went out.
They went to the edge of the village, and they gathered stones.
They made like-- they made like piles of stones.
And there were about maybe four or five students with bicycles.
You know, they came specially to beat up Jews.
When they reached the edge of my village, we were waiting for them.
We beat them up.
They left the bicycles there, and they ran away.
But of course, again, because in my village, people grew up plain, they grew up proud, they were not afraid-- I mean, of course, they were afraid.
But for a few fascists, they're not going to run.
And when I think back-- and I remember that incident, I'm very proud that at least we stood up.
And I believe that if the Jewish people in other places would have organized and stayed-- and, you know, to a certain extent-- not with the Nazis.
I'm not talking about the Nazis because what the Nazis did, that was a plan.
You couldn't defeat Nazi Germany.
France couldn't defeat-- nah, I mean.
But I'm talking local incidents, like pogroms and stuff like this-- we would've been-- we would've been OK.
Then in 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if you-- you know, there is the-- what do you call the-- excuse me.
[DRINKS]
I can just say something else.

My village-- the first people to settle in my village, I think-- I think, as far as I could-- as-- but as far as I know, not remember-- as far as I know, must've been maybe in the mid-19th century, like 1840, around the 1840s.
How do I know this?
Because I was born in Comlausa.
My mother was born in Comlausa.
My grandmother was born in Comlausa.
My grandmother's parents were born in Comlausa.
That's my great-grandfather and grandmother.
No, only my great-grandfather was born in Comlausa, not my great-grandmother.
So it goes like this.
My name is Yankl.
My mother's name is Rifka.
My grandmother's name was Leah.
My great-grandfather, who was born-- I'm talking only people who were born in Comlausa, his name was was Shloimo Leazar.
And his father-- his father's name was Mayer.
And they settled-- whether they were the first one or not-- this, I don't know.
But as I say, I could trace it back almost to the mid-19th century.
This Mayer had three children.
One was Yankl, and one son's name was Lachida.
And one son's name was Shloimo Leazar.
This Yankl, he's the one that my father inherited.
He-- because he was adopted to-- as I said before.
The ?] Lachida and Shloimo Leazar, they both had like 10, 12 children each.
So my whole village-- actually, my whole village, I would say 90% were related.
They were cousins, uncles, all related.
In other words, all the 26 families, I would probably say anywhere, 21, maybe 21, 22 families were in a somes way related-- related.
Now that doesn't mean that they got along very nicely because they were related.
I remember in shul, they used to have fights.
What was the big fight?
Usually, before Simchas Torah, it's the holiday called Hoshana Rabbah.
Hoshana Rabbah, you're supposed to stay in shul all night long because that's the night when the heavens part.
So what did the people do all night long to wait for the heavens to part?
They would elect a president.
Over there, it was known as a gabbai.
And who would be elected?
It was only a day away from Simchas Torah.
In Simchas Torah, everybody got drunk.
Whoever could give more wine on the table, when he started to drink, and he was elected president.
So most of the fights would begin then.
Why it was he elected, why wasn't he elected, and so on and so forth.
And sometimes those fights were pretty rough.
I remember we used to have a gate around my shul, made out of, like a-- like a picket fence, and they would take apart a whole fence.
They-- they needed some clubs.
But one thing I'll say, when they finished fighting, they all walked together like brothers.
Outside, you never knew there was a fight.
Once, I remember there was a fight because somebody called my grandfather [NON-ENGLISH].
Ask me what [NON-ENGLISH] is.
This is something you say Passover by the-- by the seder.
But because they called him [NON-ENGLISH] was a good enough-- a good enough reason to have a fight.
So they were fighting.
In a way, it was a way-- it's-- it was a way of letting off steam because it was a small village.
There were no-- there were no theaters, no restaurant, no-- and so-- so-- you know, they would fight.
All right, whether it's nice or not nice, but this is how it was.
This type of life was going on.
It was quiet.
It was nice.
The scenery was beautiful.
My village is in the foothills of the Carpathian Mountains.
And-- and-- and the fields were lush and green and nice.
And-- and, you know, we had to go to the forest to-- for-- for-- for wood, to make meals.
And as a child, I would go out in the-- in the-- in the woods and gather-- gather old branches to bring home for my mother to cook because there was no gas over there.
You know, we had to go and gather branches.
And the forest didn't really have good wood.
You know, they were poor forests.
But they had a lot of branches, you know.
So I would make bundles and bring it for my mother.
And this is how she would cook.
But as I say, they were-- because since didn't know any better, they were happy days.
If I had a new-- a new-- a new pair of pants or a new suit or something like this, it was a big thing.
You made a-- you made a [HEBREW].
You made a [HEBREW].
It was a big-- but most of the time, what happened, it was passed along.
In other words, I would-- when I grew up, my shirt, I would give it to my-- to my-- to my--
Brothers.

And so-- and what they would also do is take-- like, in other words, if you-- if somebody had an old coat, they would take the old coat and turn it inside out so that, you know-- and there were tailors there, you know.
And-- and-- and it was a project.
You know, just to make a coat, it would take a couple of weeks, you know?
[LAUGHS] So-- so this is how you-- you had-- you had clothes.
My mother would also make-- they would spin their own-- she would spin her own-- and not only my mother, but the entire village.
They grew flax.
I don't know if you know what flax is.
And they would put it in the water, soak it in the water.
And then-- but it's a whole process to it.
And then they would break it, and they would make flax and take the flax and put it on a long-- on a long stick, like, you know, on a long stick.
And in the end, my mother would have like a spindle.
And down at the spindle was a potato in order to-- for balance.
And she would go like-- she would go like.
She would-- she-- she-- she-- she would take the-- the flax and pull it like.
And-- and then she would spin it over here, then pull it, and she would [? spin it. ?] And-- and-- and this is how she would make thread-- thread.
And when she had enough thread, then I would have-- then she would-- I would keep my hands like this.
And she would put the flax in my both hands because then she had to make rows.
And I would keep my hands.
My-- I felt my hands are falling out from my socket because I had to keep it like this.
And she would make-- she would make, you know, balls of-- of-- of-- of this--
--material.
And then in the wintertime, they would set up like a-- like a--
